Moreover, EFRL carried the subtitle Le système totémique en Australie, and van Gennep had in 1904 published his first book on the very topic of totemism, Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar. This was a book that Marcel Mauss had proofread and contributed to, and that contained an important (if early and still developing) theoretical discussion of totemism. Durkheim knew van Gennep’s work beyond any reasonable doubt, and it is likely that Mauss felt a certain degree Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 53 of unease omitting a more thorough discussion of van Gennep. They had studied many of the texts on totemism and primitive religion together. Van Gennep published his review of EFRL in Mercure de France, on January 16, 1913 (van Gennep 2001: 92–5). EFRL is still today considered the classic work within the sociology of religion, and whether we like it or not, it is where many of our discussions start. However much we choose to deviate from EFRL, it has in many ways taken hold of our imaginary boundaries of the subject field. The question we seriously have to ask is whether this book should not be considered a genuinely false start. At the very least, when we today make reference to Durkheim’s work on religion (and use it as the standard text in our university courses) we jointly ought to include and consider van Gennep’s early critique. Van Gennep’s review of EFRL neatly sums up, in three pages, everything that he finds problematic with Durkheim’s exposé. As is well-known, EFRL is composed of two parts; one is general-theoretical, the other is monographic. The latter, starts van Gennep, is the weakest part. Van Gennep feels more than entitled to engage with this ethnographic part, for as he says, he knows each and every one of Durkheim’s sources, having used them himself in previous works. Van Gennep states quite bluntly that Durkheim demonstrates a complete lack of any critical stance towards his sources, collected by traders, police agents and priests, naively accepting their veracity. Durkheim over-states the theoretical potential of single facts and interprets freely from dubious data. It is this ‘fictional’ procedure that Durkheim falsely attributes to the ‘German’ method. 6 In every single line of the book, says van Gennep, one feels tempted to dot down a simple but legitimate question mark: ‘Is he right about what he is saying?’, ‘What are his sources?’, ‘How can we actually know that what he argues corresponds to reality?’. Van Gennep was himself an extremely meticulous note-taker and bibliographer, and as a reviewer he would confront any academic work with the same criticism: the moment it failed to be transparent about sources, their reliability and veracity, and the logical steps made in one’s argumentation, he would point it out, warning the reader that the conclusions reached could not be verified and therefore may not stand up to scrutiny. Durkheim’s book did not pass the test on this first, fundamental measure of accuracy and reliability. In other words, van Gennep was quite fundamentally challenging Durkheim on his empirical data, technical knowledge and scientific transparency. He therefore states the first part of the conclusions categorically and definitively: Within ten years, his entire systematisation of the Australian material will be completely rejected, and with them the generalizations that he has constructed on the basis of the most fragile ethnographic data I have ever seen. The view he has proposed concerning ‘primitive man’ and ‘simple’ societies is entirely erroneous. (ibid.: 92, my translation) 6 Durkheim had had a crucial stay in Germany in 1885–1886, and liked to see himself as the transmitter of German empiricism and neo-Kantianism to the French universities. Liminality and the Modern 54 This was no minor issue, since Durkheim’s project and his very academic identity rested upon a reputation of being scientific and empirically founded. What Durkheim knew of ethnography he mostly had from Mauss. His command of the material was very partial. Van Gennep, better than anyone else, was in a position to spot Durkheim’s lack of expertise in ethnography, and Durkheim’s tendency to press the ethnography into a prefabricated theoretical scheme. The plain fact is that Durkheim’s insistence upon using ‘facts’ and ‘observable social phenomena’ for theory building was simply not followed up in his own work; Arnold van Gennep pointed this out more clearly than anyone else has ever done since. And he did so without the privilege of hindsight. For that reason alone, sociologists should start to take van Gennep much more seriously. Here of course, one may legitimately come to the rescue of Durkheim: does his theoretical attempt not deserve to be taken seriously, irrespective of a superficially analysed and flawed empirical material? Van Gennep would agree: Durkheim’s general theories on totemism and on religion deserve to be considered in their own right, and are in fact full of ‘solid truths’ (ibid.: 93). Chapter V, a critique of existing theories on totemism is ‘simple, rapid, exact’ (we can be quite sure that Mauss composed most of this chapter). And yet, when Durkheim in the next chapter (on the origins of totemic beliefs) moves on to suggest his own theory, no real theory is ever proposed. What Durkheim offers, says van Gennep, is a definition of totemism (Durkheim famously proposes to see totemism as representing an anonymous and impersonal ‘force’). More than explaining anything, this definition only raises a series of question marks concerning the nature of totemism. It also misrecognizes what van Gennep had argued to be the essentially ‘energetic’ nature of religious conceptions (this is in fact yet another of van Gennep’s ideas that Mauss would later take over). Durkheim, said van Gennep, claimed to have established the ‘foundations of society’ from a single religious institution (totemism), without realizing that this was just one very specific type of classification, peculiar to this not-so-simple society.
